title: Toxic Shock Syndrome
oneLiner: Rare toxin-mediated sepsis from Staph aureus or Strep pyogenes; high mortality if missed in first hours.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Septic shock (TSS itself):** rapid-onset refractory hypotension + diffuse erythematous rash that desquamates within 1–2 weeks — if you see this, start fluids, source control, and antibiotics **now**.
- **Meningococcaemia:** non-blanching purpuric rash with petechiae — if present, give IV ceftriaxone immediately (do not wait for LP).
- **Necrotising fasciitis (especially streptococcal TSS):** severe localised pain out of proportion to exam + bullae/crepitus — needs surgical review and high-dose clindamycin + penicillin.
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal symptoms:
  - Sudden high fever (>38.9°C) + hypotension (systolic <90 mmHg) within 24–48 hours
  - Diffuse macular erythematous rash (like sunburn) that later peels on palms and soles
  - Myalgias, headache, vomiting, diarrhoea, sore throat
  - Conjunctival injection, strawberry tongue, peripheral oedema
- Key exam findings:
  - Hypotension, tachycardia, reduced urine output
  - Rash (may be subtle on dark skin — feel for warmth and check palms/soles)
  - Focus of infection: tampon (vaginal discharge), nasal packing, surgical wound, abscess, post-partum endometritis
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Sepsis from other cause** — no diffuse rash, no desquamation, different source on imaging
- **Kawasaki disease (children)** — no hypotension, bilateral conjunctival injection without exudate, cervical lymphadenopathy, coronary aneurysms
- **Scarlet fever** — fine sandpaper rash, strawberry tongue, positive throat culture for group A strep, no hypotension
- **Viral exanthem (measles, dengue)** — Koplik spots, retro-orbital pain, no severe hypotension
- **Meningococcaemia** — purpura fulminans, petechiae, rapid progression
- **Splitter:** diffuse erythematous rash + hypotension = TSS until proven otherwise; presence of purpura → meningococcaemia
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Blood pressure trend, urine output (catheter)
  - Capillary lactate (arterial or venous)
  - ECG for arrhythmia / ischaemia (troponin elevation possible)
  - Remove any obvious foreign body (tampon, nasal pack) — send for culture
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (neutrophilia, thrombocytopenia), CRP, U&Es, creatinine, LFTs, clotting (DIC screen)
  - Blood cultures (×2 before antibiotics)
  - CK (myositis), troponin (myocarditis)
  - Group A streptococcus PCR if focal infection suspected
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR (look for ARDS / pneumonia)
  - CT/MRI if source unclear (abscess, necrotising fasciitis, retained surgical products)
- **Special tests:**
  - Vaginal/cervical swabs (if menstruating) — culture for Staph aureus
  - Wound swab, throat swab, surgical site culture
  - Toxicology screen if drug-induced (e.g., NSAIDs, cocaine rarely mimic)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **CDC criteria for staphylococcal TSS:** fever ≥38.9°C, hypotension, diffuse rash, desquamation (1–2 weeks later) + involvement of ≥3 organ systems (GI, renal, hepatic, CNS, haematological)
  - **Streptococcal TSS:** definite if isolation of group A Strep from sterile site + hypotension + ≥2 organ dysfunctions; probable if non-sterile site
- **Never-miss test:** blood cultures (before antibiotics) + removal and culture of any foreign body/tampon — omission delays pathogen identification and source control
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - ABC: high-flow oxygen, IV access ×2, fluid resuscitation with crystalloid (30 mL/kg bolus, repeat if no response)
  - Vasopressors (noradrenaline) if still hypotensive after 2L fluids
  - Source control: **remove tampon** (document removal), drain abscess, debride necrotic tissue
  - Start antibiotics (see Drugs & Doses)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - ICU step-up if requires vasopressors >1h or lactate >4
  - Monitor for DIC (platelets, INR, fibrinogen), AKI (urine output, creatinine), ARDS (daily ABG, CXR)
  - Consider IVIG (if refractory shock despite fluids + vasopressors + antibiotics) — consult senior / microbiology
  - Continue source control (drainage, repeat surgical debridement as needed)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - No specific long-term therapy
  - If menstrual TSS, advise avoid tampons for at least 4–6 weeks; consider avoidance of high-absorbency products
  - If recurrent, consider decolonisation (mupirocin + chlorhexidine) — discuss with microbiology
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV normal saline or Hartmann's at 125 mL/h (maintenance) once resuscitated; adjust for renal function
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (if eGFR >30, no bleeding)
  - **Diet/NBM status:** NBM initially if surgical source or vomiting; otherwise clear fluids advance as tolerated
  - **Observation frequency:** hourly vital signs + urine output until stable, then 4-hourly
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Clindamycin** 600–900 mg IV every 8 hours (toxin-suppressing effect, regardless of sensitivities)
  - **Plus** a beta-lactam:
    - For staphylococcal TSS (MSSA): **Flucloxacillin** 2 g IV every 6 hours
    - For streptococcal TSS: **Benzylpenicillin** 2.4 g IV every 4 hours
    - If unsure (mixed / unknown): **Ceftriaxone** 2 g IV daily (cover both + Gram-negatives)
  - Duration: minimum 10–14 days IV, then switch oral based on culture and clinical response (use [check local microbiology policy])
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Vancomycin** (for MRSA) — dose: 15–20 mg/kg IV q8–12h (trough levels needed)
  - **Linezolid** if vancomycin allergy or failure (600 mg IV/PO q12h)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Clindamycin: risk of *C. difficile* colitis; use with caution in colitis history
  - Flucloxacillin: avoid in penicillin allergy; adjust dose in renal impairment (max 4 g/day if eGFR <10)
  - Benzylpenicillin: high risk of seizure with high dose in renal failure
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** all penicillins and clindamycin are predominantly hepatic — no major renal adjustment needed except flucloxacillin max dose; vancomycin requires trough monitoring
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Not adding clindamycin** — toxin suppression is key; giving only a beta-lactam without clindamycin may fail to halt toxin-mediated shock. Also: **delaying antibiotics for source removal** (remove tampon first or simultaneously, but don't wait for cultures)
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Refractory septic shock (DIC, ARDS, AKI)
  - Myocardial depression (troponin rise, arrhythmia)
  - Compartment syndrome (if necrotising fasciitis)
  - Acute kidney injury from rhabdomyolysis
- **Chronic:**
  - Rare — residual renal impairment (if prolonged AKI)
  - Recurrence (5–10% of menstrual TSS)
sections.Prognosis:
- Mortality: 5–10% for menstrual staphylococcal TSS; up to 30–50% for streptococcal TSS (especially if necrotising fasciitis or shock not reversed within 6 hours)
- Survivors: full recovery in days to weeks; desquamation resolves; no chronic organ failure unless severe ARDS/AKI
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always ask** about tampon, menstrual cup, nasal packing, surgical wound, intrauterine device, or recent childbirth — removal is part of treatment
- **Start clindamycin** *before* culture results — it stops toxin production; you can de-escalate later
- **Monitor urine output hourly** — acute kidney injury occurs early; if <0.5 mL/kg/h for 2 hours, escalate fluid and consider renal replacement
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give steroids** — no proven benefit and may worsen infection in TSS
- **Do not wait for desquamation** to diagnose — that happens days later; treat on clinical suspicion of fever + hypotension + rash
- **Do not delay source control** for antibiotics — remove tampon/nasal pack **immediately**; if focal abscess, arrange surgical drainage within hours
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** fever recurs (≥38°C), rash reappears or spreads, blood pressure drops (symptomatic hypotension), new severe localised pain (possible necrotising fasciitis)
- **Follow-up:** GP review within 48–72 hours; if menstrual TSS, gynaecology outpatient clinic in 2 weeks for counselling
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU** if:
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or sustained hypotension despite 30 mL/kg + 1 vasopressor
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (persistent after 2 hours of resuscitation)
  - GCS <13 or new confusion
  - Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for >2 hours despite fluids
  - Any focal painful area with crepitus/bullae (surgical emergency)
- **Specific thresholds for ICU referral:** need for >0.1 mcg/kg/min noradrenaline, or rising vasopressor requirement

rawMarkdown:
## Toxic Shock Syndrome
**One-liner:** Rare toxin-mediated sepsis from Staph aureus or Strep pyogenes; high mortality if missed in first hours.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Septic shock (TSS itself):** rapid-onset refractory hypotension + diffuse erythematous rash that desquamates within 1–2 weeks — if you see this, start fluids, source control, and antibiotics **now**.
- **Meningococcaemia:** non-blanching purpuric rash with petechiae — if present, give IV ceftriaxone immediately (do not wait for LP).
- **Necrotising fasciitis (especially streptococcal TSS):** severe localised pain out of proportion to exam + bullae/crepitus — needs surgical review and high-dose clindamycin + penicillin.

### Presentation
- Cardinal symptoms:
  - Sudden high fever (>38.9°C) + hypotension (systolic <90 mmHg) within 24–48 hours
  - Diffuse macular erythematous rash (like sunburn) that later peels on palms and soles
  - Myalgias, headache, vomiting, diarrhoea, sore throat
  - Conjunctival injection, strawberry tongue, peripheral oedema
- Key exam findings:
  - Hypotension, tachycardia, reduced urine output
  - Rash (may be subtle on dark skin — feel for warmth and check palms/soles)
  - Focus of infection: tampon (vaginal discharge), nasal packing, surgical wound, abscess, post-partum endometritis

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Sepsis from other cause** — no diffuse rash, no desquamation, different source on imaging
- **Kawasaki disease (children)** — no hypotension, bilateral conjunctival injection without exudate, cervical lymphadenopathy, coronary aneurysms
- **Scarlet fever** — fine sandpaper rash, strawberry tongue, positive throat culture for group A strep, no hypotension
- **Viral exanthem (measles, dengue)** — Koplik spots, retro-orbital pain, no severe hypotension
- **Meningococcaemia** — purpura fulminans, petechiae, rapid progression
- **Splitter:** diffuse erythematous rash + hypotension = TSS until proven otherwise; presence of purpura → meningococcaemia

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Blood pressure trend, urine output (catheter)
  - Capillary lactate (arterial or venous)
  - ECG for arrhythmia / ischaemia (troponin elevation possible)
  - Remove any obvious foreign body (tampon, nasal pack) — send for culture
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (neutrophilia, thrombocytopenia), CRP, U&Es, creatinine, LFTs, clotting (DIC screen)
  - Blood cultures (×2 before antibiotics)
  - CK (myositis), troponin (myocarditis)
  - Group A streptococcus PCR if focal infection suspected
- **Imaging:**
  - CXR (look for ARDS / pneumonia)
  - CT/MRI if source unclear (abscess, necrotising fasciitis, retained surgical products)
- **Special tests:**
  - Vaginal/cervical swabs (if menstruating) — culture for Staph aureus
  - Wound swab, throat swab, surgical site culture
  - Toxicology screen if drug-induced (e.g., NSAIDs, cocaine rarely mimic)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - **CDC criteria for staphylococcal TSS:** fever ≥38.9°C, hypotension, diffuse rash, desquamation (1–2 weeks later) + involvement of ≥3 organ systems (GI, renal, hepatic, CNS, haematological)
  - **Streptococcal TSS:** definite if isolation of group A Strep from sterile site + hypotension + ≥2 organ dysfunctions; probable if non-sterile site
- **Never-miss test:** blood cultures (before antibiotics) + removal and culture of any foreign body/tampon — omission delays pathogen identification and source control

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - ABC: high-flow oxygen, IV access ×2, fluid resuscitation with crystalloid (30 mL/kg bolus, repeat if no response)
  - Vasopressors (noradrenaline) if still hypotensive after 2L fluids
  - Source control: **remove tampon** (document removal), drain abscess, debride necrotic tissue
  - Start antibiotics (see Drugs & Doses)
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - ICU step-up if requires vasopressors >1h or lactate >4
  - Monitor for DIC (platelets, INR, fibrinogen), AKI (urine output, creatinine), ARDS (daily ABG, CXR)
  - Consider IVIG (if refractory shock despite fluids + vasopressors + antibiotics) — consult senior / microbiology
  - Continue source control (drainage, repeat surgical debridement as needed)
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - No specific long-term therapy
  - If menstrual TSS, advise avoid tampons for at least 4–6 weeks; consider avoidance of high-absorbency products
  - If recurrent, consider decolonisation (mupirocin + chlorhexidine) — discuss with microbiology
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** IV normal saline or Hartmann's at 125 mL/h (maintenance) once resuscitated; adjust for renal function
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily (if eGFR >30, no bleeding)
  - **Diet/NBM status:** NBM initially if surgical source or vomiting; otherwise clear fluids advance as tolerated
  - **Observation frequency:** hourly vital signs + urine output until stable, then 4-hourly

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents:**
  - **Clindamycin** 600–900 mg IV every 8 hours (toxin-suppressing effect, regardless of sensitivities)
  - **Plus** a beta-lactam:
    - For staphylococcal TSS (MSSA): **Flucloxacillin** 2 g IV every 6 hours
    - For streptococcal TSS: **Benzylpenicillin** 2.4 g IV every 4 hours
    - If unsure (mixed / unknown): **Ceftriaxone** 2 g IV daily (cover both + Gram-negatives)
  - Duration: minimum 10–14 days IV, then switch oral based on culture and clinical response (use [check local microbiology policy])
- **Common alternatives:**
  - **Vancomycin** (for MRSA) — dose: 15–20 mg/kg IV q8–12h (trough levels needed)
  - **Linezolid** if vancomycin allergy or failure (600 mg IV/PO q12h)
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**
  - Clindamycin: risk of *C. difficile* colitis; use with caution in colitis history
  - Flucloxacillin: avoid in penicillin allergy; adjust dose in renal impairment (max 4 g/day if eGFR <10)
  - Benzylpenicillin: high risk of seizure with high dose in renal failure
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** all penicillins and clindamycin are predominantly hepatic — no major renal adjustment needed except flucloxacillin max dose; vancomycin requires trough monitoring
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Not adding clindamycin** — toxin suppression is key; giving only a beta-lactam without clindamycin may fail to halt toxin-mediated shock. Also: **delaying antibiotics for source removal** (remove tampon first or simultaneously, but don't wait for cultures)

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Refractory septic shock (DIC, ARDS, AKI)
  - Myocardial depression (troponin rise, arrhythmia)
  - Compartment syndrome (if necrotising fasciitis)
  - Acute kidney injury from rhabdomyolysis
- **Chronic:**
  - Rare — residual renal impairment (if prolonged AKI)
  - Recurrence (5–10% of menstrual TSS)

### Prognosis
- Mortality: 5–10% for menstrual staphylococcal TSS; up to 30–50% for streptococcal TSS (especially if necrotising fasciitis or shock not reversed within 6 hours)
- Survivors: full recovery in days to weeks; desquamation resolves; no chronic organ failure unless severe ARDS/AKI

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always ask** about tampon, menstrual cup, nasal packing, surgical wound, intrauterine device, or recent childbirth — removal is part of treatment
- **Start clindamycin** *before* culture results — it stops toxin production; you can de-escalate later
- **Monitor urine output hourly** — acute kidney injury occurs early; if <0.5 mL/kg/h for 2 hours, escalate fluid and consider renal replacement

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give steroids** — no proven benefit and may worsen infection in TSS
- **Do not wait for desquamation** to diagnose — that happens days later; treat on clinical suspicion of fever + hypotension + rash
- **Do not delay source control** for antibiotics — remove tampon/nasal pack **immediately**; if focal abscess, arrange surgical drainage within hours

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** fever recurs (≥38°C), rash reappears or spreads, blood pressure drops (symptomatic hypotension), new severe localised pain (possible necrotising fasciitis)
- **Follow-up:** GP review within 48–72 hours; if menstrual TSS, gynaecology outpatient clinic in 2 weeks for counselling

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU** if:
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or sustained hypotension despite 30 mL/kg + 1 vasopressor
  - Lactate >4 mmol/L (persistent after 2 hours of resuscitation)
  - GCS <13 or new confusion
  - Urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h for >2 hours despite fluids
  - Any focal painful area with crepitus/bullae (surgical emergency)
- **Specific thresholds for ICU referral:** need for >0.1 mcg/kg/min noradrenaline, or rising vasopressor requirement
